frameworks.name: Trial-to-Activation Rebate Structure
frameworks.definition: Two-step activation incentive: charge upfront, then rebate or waive the next billing period if the user completes a defined first-value action within the trial window.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi advises Drew (fence-visualization SaaS, ~$500K ARR) that scaling paid ads before fixing activation-driven churn is pointless: revenue acquired will simply exit through the same leaky back-end, making additional ad spend a negative-ROI activity until activation is solved.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Monthly churn caused primarily by non-activation (users who never experience first value moment)
Paid acquisition is the growth channel
claims.mechanism: Acquired customers who never activate churn at the same rate regardless of ad volume; increasing acquisition speed without reducing activation churn increases absolute churn dollars proportionally, yielding zero net retention gain.
bounded_by: Does not apply when churn is caused by product-quality issues unrelated to activation

claims.text: Alex Hormozi tells Drew that an 8% monthly churn rate is too high for a SaaS to be a healthy business, and that the target should be below 2% per month (roughly 80%+ annual retention) to justify scaling.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: monthly churn rate as a threshold for SaaS health

claims.text: Alex Hormozi explains that for Drew's fence-visualization SaaS, the binding constraint on churn is activation, not product quality: users who sell a fence using the app within the first 10 days virtually never cancel, while users who do not use it within that window almost always churn.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: SaaS where the primary value is demonstrated in a single first-use action (e.g., a sales tool used in-field)
claims.mechanism: First-use of the product delivers a tangible outcome (a sold fence) that creates immediate ROI perception; without this experience the user has no emotional or financial reason to continue paying.

numbers.axis: days to first product use and subsequent retention

anchors.region: used product to sell fence within 10 days
did not use product within 10 days

bounded_by: Applies to tools where the value is demonstrated in a discrete, observable first action; may not generalize to SaaS where value accumulates over weeks

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that Drew replace a passive free trial (credit card attached, cancel anytime) with a free-trial-with-penalty structure, because an unpenalized trial gives users no financial reason to take the activation action.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: SaaS with a free trial period
Activation requires a specific first-use behavior from the user
claims.mechanism: A penalty (or upfront charge) creates a loss-aversion incentive that motivates the user to take the activation action; without a penalty the trial removes financial pressure and delays or eliminates first use.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi proposes a specific rebate mechanic for Drew: charge $199 upfront, then rebate or skip the second billing month if the user completes 2-3 in-field uses within the first 10 days, because this aligns the business's retention interest with the user's cost-recovery interest.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: SaaS tool with a discrete, trackable first-value action
claims.mechanism: Charging upfront increases commitment; offering a rebate contingent on completing the activation action converts the rebate into an activation incentive; users motivated by cost-recovery attempt the action and in doing so discover the product's value, anchoring retention.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi points out that Drew's reported $130 CAC is the cost per trial signup, not per paying retained customer, and that the true CAC is closer to $500 once trial-to-conversion rate (approximately 1 in 3-4 trials converting) is factored in.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: cost to acquire a customer not a trial
conditions: Businesses using a free or low-friction trial before paid conversion
Reported CAC based on cost-per-trial rather than cost-per-retained-customer
claims.mechanism: Trial signups do not equate to paid customers; the true CAC must account for the trial-to-paid conversion rate (divide cost per trial by the conversion rate).

numbers.axis: CAC calculation basis for fence-visualization SaaS (Drew's business, transcript date ~2025-2026)

anchors.region: true cost per acquired paying customer

bounded_by: Estimate based on Drew's stated conversion ratio; actual figure requires precise cohort tracking

claims.text: Alex Hormozi instructs Drew to measure conversion rate from trial to paid separately from post-conversion churn, because blending trial churn into overall churn inflates the apparent churn rate and obscures the true retention of paying customers.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Subscription SaaS with a free or low-friction trial gate before paid conversion
Business is small enough that trial volume is a large fraction of the active user base
claims.mechanism: Trial users are a qualitatively different cohort from paying customers; aggregating their dropout into a single churn figure makes the retention of the paying customer segment appear worse than it is, leading to misdirected product and pricing decisions.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi advises Drew to book a live onboarding session immediately after a trial signup and use BAMFAM (book a meeting from a meeting) to ensure the user attempts the activation action under guided support, because a supported first use is far more likely to succeed than an unguided self-serve attempt.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: BAMFAM, like right to an onboarding
conditions: SaaS where the value requires a field action (e.g., showing a client a visualization in their yard)
User needs guided setup or demonstration to complete first activation
claims.mechanism: Scheduled onboarding creates a commitment device and ensures hands-on guidance during the highest-risk dropout window (the first 10 days); completion of the guided session dramatically increases the probability of the user reaching the first-value moment.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi tells Drew that all effort, incentive, and operational attention should be concentrated on activation because once the first-value moment (selling a fence with the app) is achieved, the rest of the retention machine works on its own.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: all the effort all the incentive goes there
conditions: SaaS product where one discrete first-use event is strongly predictive of long-term retention
claims.mechanism: The first value moment eliminates the user's uncertainty about whether the product delivers ROI; once ROI is experienced, cancellation motivation drops near zero and standard product quality sustains retention without additional incentives.
bounded_by: Assumes the product delivers real value post-activation; if the product fails to deliver ongoing value after the first use, activation alone will not sustain retention long-term

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that offering a free trial without an attached penalty gives users no incentive to take the activation action, because free access removes financial pressure without replacing it with behavioral pressure.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: there's no reason for them to do it
conditions: Free trial with credit card attached, cancel-anytime
Activation requires a specific behavioral effort from the user (not passive content consumption)
claims.mechanism: Free access combined with low switching cost removes the loss-aversion trigger; users can defer the activation action indefinitely, and inertia causes them to cancel rather than act.

examples.text: Drew's fence-visualization SaaS (approximately $500K ARR at time of interview) ran a 10-day free trial with credit card attached and cancel-anytime policy. Users who placed a fence visualization in at least one customer's yard within the trial window almost never canceled; users who did not attempt the feature within 10 days churned at a rate that drove an overall 8% monthly churn. Alex Hormozi diagnosed this as a trial incentive gap: the free trial gave no financial reason to attempt the feature, so most trial users never experienced the product's core value and passively churned.

Drew reported a $130 cost-per-lead from Facebook instant-form ads, noting that a particularly strong ad had produced leads at $1.50 each. Alex Hormozi cautioned Drew not to rely on the $1.50 figure and pointed out that the $130 figure itself was likely the cost per trial signup rather than the cost per paying retained customer. With roughly 8 trials converting to 3-4 paying customers, the true CAC was closer to $500, not $130 — a 3-4x understatement that would distort any decision about whether to scale ads.

practices.text: Do not scale paid advertising for a subscription product until monthly churn is below 2%; every dollar spent on acquisition while churn is high exits through the back-end.
Charge upfront before the trial period rather than attaching a card for future billing; upfront payment increases user commitment to taking the activation action.
Track trial-to-paid conversion rate and post-conversion churn as two separate metrics; never blend trial dropout into the churn figure used for business health assessment.
Set the rebate or free-month threshold at the number of activation actions that statistically predicts retention (e.g., 2-3 fence visualizations placed), not an arbitrary engagement figure.
Book a live onboarding call immediately upon trial signup and BAMFAM a follow-up to ensure the user attempts the activation action before the trial window expires.
Be transparent with new users about why the rebate or incentive exists: explain that you know once they use the product they will stay, and you are paying them to try it.
